---
title: "5.2.1 What is digital currency?"
subtitle: "Digital currency · P186-P187"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.74em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)

## Digital Currency

### No physical form

<span class="term">Digital currency</span> (数字货币) exists purely in a digital format.

It has <span class="mark">no physical form</span> unlike conventional <span class="term">fiat currency</span>, for example $, £, €, and ¥.

Digital currency is an accepted form of payment to pay for goods or services.

## Fiat Currency

### Backed by governments and banks

Fiat is a Latin word meaning "let it be done".

Conventional currency is backed by governments and banks rather than being linked to gold or silver reserves, so it is referred to as <span class="term">fiat currency</span>.

## Digital Transfers

### Money as data

As with cash or credit/debit cards, digital currency can be transferred between various accounts when carrying out transactions.

It has made it possible to bank online or via a smartphone app because money only exists as <span class="mark">data on a computer system</span>, but it can be transferred into physical cash if needed.

## Central Banking

### Traditional digital currency

Digital currency relies on a <span class="term">central banking system</span>.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 5.6 Digital currency screenshot placeholder
:::

The problem with centralisation is maintaining <span class="mark">confidentiality and security</span>.

## Cryptocurrency

### Decentralisation

One example of digital currency, known as <span class="term">cryptocurrency</span> (加密货币), has essentially overcome confidentiality and security issues by introducing <span class="term">decentralisation</span>.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 5.7 Cryptocurrency and decentralisation screenshot placeholder
:::

## Cryptocurrency Features

### Tracking and control

::: {.tight-list}
- Cryptocurrency uses <span class="term">cryptography</span> to track transactions.
- Traditional digital currencies are regulated by central banks and governments.
- Cryptocurrency has no state control and rules are set by the cryptocurrency community.
- Cryptocurrency transactions are publicly available, so transactions can be tracked and the amount of money in the system is monitored.
- The cryptocurrency system works within a <span class="term">blockchain network</span>.
:::

## Classroom Check

### Digital currency and cryptocurrency

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- digital currency exists purely in digital format and has no physical form
- it is accepted as payment and can be transferred between accounts
- traditional digital currency relies on a central banking system
- centralisation creates confidentiality and security issues
- cryptocurrency uses cryptography and decentralisation
- cryptocurrency transactions are publicly available and work within a blockchain network
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Blockchaining

The next deck explains how a blockchain stores and checks transaction data.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)
